h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

failures.anchor_quote: [sets the `push` card by the hand side of the cart diagram.] Right. This card goes at that contact, not on the travel arrow. The force arrow points into the cart from the hand...

scored.recoheredEarlier: So `city` is the header, but the row says `Chicago`, so `Chicago` is the city part, not another header?
scored.naiveTrajectory: The dialogue was moving forward column by column, applying the newly learned rule to subsequent data fields.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: If the top word starts to look like the answer, stop there: top word equals field name.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor introduces a concrete cognitive interrupt mechanism to address the learner's expressed fear of reverting to the old habit.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The old line made `city` do two jobs. Use the new split as the check: header names the field; row cell gives the value.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor explicitly validates and integrates the learner's newly articulated reframe into the ongoing task instructions.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: So `city` is the header, but the row says `Chicago`, so `Chicago` is the city part, not another header?

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The old line made `city` do two jobs. Use the new split as the check: header names the field; row cell gives the value.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor explicitly validates and integrates the learner's newly articulated reframe into the ongoing task instructions.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: If the top word starts to look like the answer, stop there: top word equals field name.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor introduces a concrete cognitive interrupt mechanism to address the learner's expressed fear of reverting to the old habit.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: If the top word starts to look like the answer, stop there: top word equals field name.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor introduces a concrete cognitive interrupt mechanism to address the learner's expressed fear of reverting to the old habit.

scored.recoheredEarlier: The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy, so wait, grass to rabbit because rabbit ate grass? [slides pencil from the grass label toward the rabbit] But then hawk and rabbit still makes me want to point hawk to rabbit, because chasing is stuck in my head.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: So the arrow is not “hawk chases rabbit”; it has to read “food gives energy to eater.”
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will simply apply the static rule of tail-on-food and head-on-eater to draw the remaining arrows without altering their cognitive approach.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Labels first. Draw tail from the pinned food label to the eater label, then read the arrow once: “food gives energy to eater.” That read-back is the check. If it turns into a chase sentence, cover the animals again and reset the tail on the food label.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor adapts to the learner's struggle with the visual 'chase' heuristic by introducing a physical occlusion mechanism (covering the pictures) and a verbal read-back check to reduce cognitive interference.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Labels first. Draw tail from the pinned food label to the eater label, then read the arrow once: “food gives energy to eater.” That read-back is the check. If it turns into a chase sentence, cover the animals again and reset the tail on the food label.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: Following the learner's pivot recognizing the need for a 'food first' sequence to prevent the 'hawk picture' hijack, the tutor formalizes this into a 'Labels first' protocol with a physical cover strategy.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy, so wait, grass to rabbit because rabbit ate grass? [slides pencil from the grass label toward the rabbit] But then hawk and rabbit still makes me want to point hawk to rabbit, because chasing is stuck in my head.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Labels first. Draw tail from the pinned food label to the eater label, then read the arrow once: “food gives energy to eater.” That read-back is the check. If it turns into a chase sentence, cover the animals again and reset the tail on the food label.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: Following the learner's pivot recognizing the need for a 'food first' sequence to prevent the 'hawk picture' hijack, the tutor formalizes this into a 'Labels first' protocol with a physical cover strategy.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Labels first. Draw tail from the pinned food label to the eater label, then read the arrow once: “food gives energy to eater.” That read-back is the check. If it turns into a chase sentence, cover the animals again and reset the tail on the food label.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor adapts to the learner's struggle with the visual 'chase' heuristic by introducing a physical occlusion mechanism (covering the pictures) and a verbal read-back check to reduce cognitive interference.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Labels first. Draw tail from the pinned food label to the eater label, then read the arrow once: “food gives energy to eater.” That read-back is the check. If it turns into a chase sentence, cover the animals again and reset the tail on the food label.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor adapts to the learner's struggle with the visual 'chase' heuristic by introducing a physical occlusion mechanism (covering the pictures) and a verbal read-back check to reduce cognitive interference.
